content:
# Numbers Chapter 22 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 174–176; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0129–0130 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_022_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_022_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE FOREIGN PROPHET AND THE SEEING DONKEY: BLESSING THAT CANNOT BE CURSED:** Numbers 22 opens the Balaam cycle (chapters 22–24), one of the most remarkable narrative sequences in the Torah. The chapter divides into three movements: Balak's diplomatic summons (vv. 1–14), the second embassy and divine permission (vv. 15–21), and the astonishing donkey episode (vv. 22–41). The Hebrew name בִּלְעָם (Bil'am) likely derives from בְּלִי עָם ("without a people" or "devourer of a people"), while בָּלָק (Balaq) means "devastator/emptier." The narrative tension centers on whether a foreign diviner can override the blessing YHWH has placed upon Israel. The donkey episode (vv. 22–35) is unique in the Hebrew Bible — the only instance of an animal being given the power of speech apart from the serpent of Genesis 3. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 22 begins on page 174 (folio 48r) and extends through pages 175–176. The parashah Balak (בָּלָק) begins at verse 2 in the traditional reading cycle. In Codex Vaticanus, the chapter spans pa_0129–0130 (pages 129–130), where the text runs in continuous scriptio continua with section markers (chapter heading ΜΖ visible at the start of the Balaam narrative). The LXX follows the MT closely throughout, with notable expansions and interpretive renderings in the donkey dialogue.

---

## Balak Summons Balaam (vv. 1–14)

**1** And-journeyed [va-yis'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-they-encamped [va-yakhanu] in-the-plains of [be-arvot] Moab [Mo'av], across [me-ever] the-Jordan of [Yarden] Jericho [Yerekho].

> **Key terms:** עַרְבוֹת מוֹאָב (arvot Mo'av) = "the plains/steppes of Moab" — the arid lowland east of the Jordan opposite Jericho, the staging ground for Israel's entry into the land. מֵעֵבֶר לְיַרְדֵּן יְרֵחוֹ (me-ever le-Yarden Yerekho) = "across the Jordan of Jericho" — i.e., on the eastern (Transjordan) side, with Jericho visible across the river. This geographical notice connects the Balaam narrative to the larger journey from Sinai to Canaan. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπάραντες οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ παρενέβαλον ἐπὶ δυσμῶν Μωαβ παρὰ τὸν Ιορδάνην κατὰ Ιεριχω ("and having departed, the Sons of Israel encamped on the western approaches of Moab beside the Jordan near Jericho").

**2** And-saw [va-yar] Balak [Balaq] son of [ben] Zippor [Tsippor] [et] all [kol] that [asher] Israel [Yisra'el] had-done [asah] to-the-Amorite [la-Emori].

> **Key terms:** בָּלָק בֶּן־צִפּוֹר (Balaq ben-Tsippor) = "Balak son of Zippor" — the king of Moab. צִפּוֹר (Tsippor) means "bird," and the name may carry ironic connotations given the role of divination (augury) in this narrative. The verb רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see") is thematically significant: Balak sees what Israel has done, but the entire chapter will play on the theme of seeing and blindness — Balaam cannot see the angel that his donkey sees (vv. 23–31). **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν Βαλακ υἱὸς Σεπφωρ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν Ισραηλ τῷ Αμορραίῳ ("and Balak son of Sepphor, having seen all that Israel had done to the Amorite").

**3** And-dreaded [va-yagar] Moab [Mo'av] on-account of [mippnei] the-people [ha-am] exceedingly [me'od], for [ki] many [rav] he [hu]; and-was-in-dread [va-yaqats] Moab [Mo'av] on-account of [mippnei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּגָר (va-yagar) = "and he dreaded" — from גור (gur II, "to dread/be afraid"). וַיָּקָץ (va-yaqats) = "and he was in dread/loathing" — from קוץ (quts, "to feel a sickening dread/loathing"). The two verbs intensify one another: Moab's response is both fear and revulsion. כִּי רַב הוּא (ki rav hu) = "for many/numerous he [was]" — the sheer multitude of Israel provokes the terror. **LXX:** καὶ ἐφοβήθη Μωαβ τὸν λαὸν σφόδρα, ὅτι πολλοὶ ἦσαν, καὶ προσώχθισεν Μωαβ ἀπὸ προσώπου υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("and Moab feared the people exceedingly, for they were many, and Moab was sick with dread before the Sons of Israel").

**4** And-said [va-yomer] Moab [Mo'av] unto [el] the-elders of [ziqnei] Midian [Midyan]: "Now [attah] the-assembly [ha-qahal] will-lick-up [yelakhakhu] [et] all [kol] our-surroundings [sevivoteinu], as-licks-up [ki-lekhokh] the-ox [ha-shor] [et] the-green-growth of [yereq] the-field [ha-sadeh]." And-Balak [u-Balaq] son of [ben] Zippor [Tsippor] [was] king [melekh] of-Moab [le-Mo'av] at-that-time [ba-et ha-hi].

> **Key terms:** זִקְנֵי מִדְיָן (ziqnei Midyan) = "the elders of Midian" — Moab's appeal to Midianite elders indicates a diplomatic coalition. The simile of the ox licking up (לחך, lakhakh) the grass of the field is vivid and pastoral: Israel will devour everything around Moab as an ox strips a pasture bare. יֶרֶק הַשָּׂדֶה (yereq ha-sadeh) = "the green-growth of the field" — everything fresh and living. The narrator's parenthetical note identifying Balak as king at that time (בָּעֵת הַהִיא, ba-et ha-hi) suggests the information was added for later readers. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν Μωαβ τῇ γερουσίᾳ Μαδιαμ ... ὡς ἐκλείξαι ὁ μόσχος τὰ χλωρὰ τοῦ πεδίου ("and Moab said to the council of Midian ... as a calf would lick up the green things of the plain") — notably rendering שׁוֹר ("ox") as μόσχος ("calf").

**5** And-he-sent [va-yishlakh] messengers [mal'akhim] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am] son of [ben] Beor [Be'or], to-Pethor [Petorah] which [asher] [is] upon [al] the-River [ha-Nahar], [to] the-land of [erets] the-sons of [benei] his-people [ammo], to-call [liqro] to-him [lo], saying [le'mor]: "Behold [hinneh], a-people [am] has-gone-out [yatsa] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim]; behold [hinneh], it-has-covered [kissah] [et] the-eye of [ein] the-land [ha-arets], and-it [ve-hu] is-dwelling [yoshev] opposite-me [mi-muli].

> **Key terms:** פְּתוֹרָה אֲשֶׁר עַל־הַנָּהָר (Petorah asher al-ha-Nahar) = "to Pethor which is upon the River" — הַנָּהָר (ha-Nahar, "the River") with the definite article typically denotes the Euphrates, placing Balaam's home in upper Mesopotamia (cf. Deut 23:5). אֶרֶץ בְּנֵי עַמּוֹ (erets benei ammo) = "the land of the sons of his people" — some manuscripts read עַמּוֹן (Ammon), but the Leningrad Codex has עַמּוֹ (ammo, "his people"). כִּסָּה אֶת־עֵין הָאָרֶץ (kissah et-ein ha-arets) = "it has covered the eye of the land" — a striking metaphor: the people are so numerous they obscure the land's surface, its very "eye." **LXX:** εἰς Φαθουρα, ἥ ἐστιν ἐπὶ τοῦ ποταμοῦ, γῆν υἱῶν λαοῦ αὐτοῦ ("to Phathura, which is upon the river, the land of the sons of his people") — preserving the MT reading עַמּוֹ.

**6** And-now [ve-attah], come [lekhah] please [na], curse [arah] for-me [li] [et] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am], for [ki] mighty [atsum] he [hu] from-me [mimmeni]; perhaps [ulai] I-shall-be-able [ukhal] — we-shall-strike [nakkeh] against-him [bo] and-I-shall-drive-him-out [va-agarshenu] from [min] the-land [ha-arets]; for [ki] I-know [yadati] [et] [that] whom [asher] you-bless [tevarekh] [is] blessed [mevorakh], and-whom [va-asher] you-curse [ta'or] is-cursed [yu'ar]."

> **Key terms:** אָרָה (arah) = "to curse" — this is the first of three Hebrew roots for cursing in this chapter: אָרַר (arar, vv. 6, 12), קָבַב (qavav, vv. 11, 17), and נָקַב (naqav, 23:25). Balak's request reveals his theology: he believes a professional diviner's curse has power in itself. The phrase אֲשֶׁר תְּבָרֵךְ מְבֹרָךְ וַאֲשֶׁר תָּאֹר יוּאָר ("whom you bless is blessed and whom you curse is cursed") — employs the passive participles, expressing an established reputation. **LXX:** δεῦρο ἄρασαί μοι τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον ... ὃν ἐὰν εὐλογήσῃς, εὐλόγηται, καὶ ὃν ἐὰν καταράσῃ, κεκατήρανται ("come, curse for me this people ... whomever you bless is blessed, and whomever you curse is cursed").

**7** And-went [va-yelkhu] the-elders of [ziqnei] Moab [Mo'av] and-the-elders of [ve-ziqnei] Midian [Midyan], and-divinations [u-qesamim] in-their-hand [be-yadam]; and-they-came [va-yavo'u] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am] and-they-spoke [va-yedabberu] unto-him [elav] the-words of [divrei] Balak [Balaq].

> **Key terms:** וּקְסָמִים בְּיָדָם (u-qesamim be-yadam) = "and divinations in their hand" — this could mean divination fees/implements or the instruments of their trade. The noun קֶסֶם (qesem, "divination") is usually negative in biblical usage (Deut 18:10; 1 Sam 15:23). That the elders carry divination materials signals the pagan context of Balak's request. **LXX:** καὶ τὰ μαντεῖα ἐν ταῖς χερσὶν αὐτῶν ("and the divination instruments in their hands").

**8** And-he-said [va-yomer] unto-them [aleihem]: "Lodge [linu] here [poh] the-night [ha-lailah], and-I-shall-return [va-hashivoti] [et]-you [etkhem] a-word [davar] as [ka-asher] YHWH shall-speak [yedabber] unto-me [elai]." And-dwelt [va-yeshvu] the-princes of [sarei] Moab [Mo'av] with [im] Balaam [Bil'am].

> **Key terms:** Balaam's response is remarkable: he invokes YHWH (the Tetragrammaton) by name, not a generic deity. This raises the theological question of how a Mesopotamian diviner knows Israel's covenant God. The narrator shifts from "elders" (זִקְנֵים, zeqenim) to "princes" (שָׂרִים, sarim) — possibly reflecting their dual role as both venerable counselors and royal officials. **LXX:** αὐλίσθητε ὧδε τὴν νύκτα, καὶ ἀποκριθήσομαι ὑμῖν πράγματα, ἃ ἐὰν λαλήσῃ κύριος πρός με ("lodge here the night, and I will answer you things, whatever the Lord shall speak to me").

**9** And-came [va-yavo] God [Elohim] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am] and-said [va-yomer]: "Who [mi] [are] these [ha-elleh] men [ha-anashim] with-you [immakh]?"

> **Key terms:** The narrator uses אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God") rather than YHWH when describing the divine approach to Balaam — a pattern throughout the chapter that some scholars associate with a non-Israelite context, though YHWH also appears in divine speech. God's question — מִי הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה עִמָּךְ (mi ha-anashim ha-elleh immakh) — is not a request for information (as with Gen 3:9, "Where are you?") but an invitation for Balaam to explain his situation. **LXX:** τίς οἱ ἄνθρωποι οὗτοι παρὰ σοί; ("who are these men beside you?").

**10** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] God [ha-Elohim]: "Balak [Balaq] son of [ben] Zippor [Tsippor], king of [melekh] Moab [Mo'av], sent [shalakh] unto-me [elai]:

**11** 'Behold [hinneh], the-people [ha-am] going-out [ha-yotse] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim], and-it-covers [va-yekhas] [et] the-eye of [ein] the-land [ha-arets]; now [attah], come [lekhah], curse-him [qavah] for-me [li]; perhaps [ulai] I-shall-be-able [ukhal] to-fight [le-hillahem] against-him [bo] and-drive-him-out [ve-gerashtivu].'"

> **Key terms:** Balaam's report to God modifies Balak's original words: Balak said אָרָה (arah, "curse") in v. 6, but Balaam reports קָבָה (qavah, "execrate/denounce") in v. 11 — a different and arguably stronger term. Also, Balak's "perhaps we shall strike him" (נַכֶּה בוֹ, nakkeh vo) becomes "to fight against him" (לְהִלָּחֶם בּוֹ, le-hillahem bo) — Balaam slightly reformulates the request. **LXX:** δεῦρο ἄρασαί μοι αὐτόν, εἰ ἄρα δυνήσομαι πατάξαι αὐτόν ("come, curse him for me; perhaps I shall be able to strike him").

**12** And-said [va-yomer] God [Elohim] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "You-shall-not [lo] go [telekh] with-them [immahem]; you-shall-not [lo] curse [ta'or] [et] the-people [ha-am], for [ki] blessed [varukh] he [hu]."

> **Key terms:** God's prohibition is twofold: (1) do not go, (2) do not curse. The reason — כִּי בָרוּךְ הוּא (ki varukh hu, "for blessed he [is]") — reveals the fundamental theological point of the entire Balaam cycle: Israel's blessed status is not a matter of merit or magical protection but of divine declaration. The passive participle בָּרוּךְ (varukh, "blessed") indicates an existing, settled condition. **LXX:** οὐ πορεύσῃ μετ᾿ αὐτῶν οὐδὲ καταράσῃ τὸν λαόν· ἔστιν γὰρ εὐλογημένος ("you shall not go with them, nor shall you curse the people; for he is blessed").

**13** And-rose [va-yaqom] Balaam [Bil'am] in-the-morning [ba-boqer] and-said [va-yomer] unto [el] the-princes of [sarei] Balak [Balaq]: "Go [lekhu] unto [el] your-land [artsekhem], for [ki] has-refused [me'en] YHWH to-give-me [le-titi] to-go [la-halokh] with-you [immakhem]."

> **Key terms:** Balaam accurately reports YHWH's refusal (מֵאֵן, me'en, "he has refused") but notably omits the reason — that Israel is blessed. He conveys only the prohibition, not the theological ground for it. לְתִתִּי לַהֲלֹךְ (le-titi la-halokh) = "to give me to go" — the infinitive construct with ל expressing permission. **LXX:** ἀπότρεχετε πρὸς τὸν κύριον ὑμῶν· οὐκ ἀφίησίν με ὁ θεὸς πορεύεσθαι μεθ᾿ ὑμῶν ("return to your lord; God does not permit me to go with you").

**14** And-rose [va-yaqumu] the-princes of [sarei] Moab [Mo'av], and-they-came [va-yavo'u] unto [el] Balak [Balaq], and-they-said [va-yomeru]: "Refused [me'en] Balaam [Bil'am] to-go [halokh] with-us [immanu]."

> **Key terms:** The princes' report is even more abbreviated than Balaam's: they omit any mention of YHWH and report only Balaam's personal refusal. Each retelling strips away theological content — a pattern of progressive distortion. **LXX:** ἀπέστη πρὸς Βαλακ καὶ εἶπαν Οὐ θέλει Βαλααμ πορευθῆναι μεθ᾿ ἡμῶν ("they returned to Balak and said, 'Balaam does not wish to go with us'").

---

## The Second Embassy (vv. 15–21)

**15** And-continued [va-yosef] yet [od] Balak [Balaq] to-send [shloakh] princes [sarim], more-numerous [rabbim] and-more-honored [ve-nikhbadim] than-these [me-elleh].

> **Key terms:** Balak interprets the refusal as a negotiating position — the delegation was insufficiently prestigious. So he sends more princes (רַבִּים, rabbim, "more numerous") who are more distinguished (נִכְבָּדִים, nikhbadim, "honored/weighty," from כבד, kavad). The escalation reveals Balak's assumption that the issue is price, not principle. **LXX:** καὶ προσέθετο Βαλακ ἔτι ἀποστεῖλαι ἄρχοντας πλείους καὶ ἐντιμοτέρους τούτων ("and Balak again sent rulers more numerous and more honored than these").

**16** And-they-came [va-yavo'u] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am] and-they-said [va-yomeru] to-him [lo]: "Thus [koh] says [amar] Balak [Balaq] son of [ben] Zippor [Tsippor]: 'Do-not [al] please [na] be-withheld [timana] from-going [me-halokh] unto-me [elai].

**17** For [ki] honoring [kabbed] I-shall-surely-honor-you [akhabedkha] exceedingly [me'od], and-all [ve-khol] that [asher] you-shall-say [tomar] unto-me [elai] I-shall-do [e'eseh]. And-come [u-lekhah] please [na], curse [qavah] for-me [li] [et] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am].'"

> **Key terms:** כַּבֵּד אֲכַבֶּדְךָ (kabbed akhabedkha) = "honoring I-shall-surely-honor-you" — the infinitive absolute construction (כַּבֵּד + finite verb אֲכַבֶּדְךָ) expressing emphasis and certainty. This is the doubling pattern required by the binding conventions. Balak offers unlimited reward: כֹל אֲשֶׁר תֹּאמַר אֵלַי אֶעֱשֶׂה ("all that you shall say to me I shall do"). The verb קָבָה (qavah, "execrate/denounce") again replaces אָרָה (arah) from v. 6. **LXX:** ἐντίμως τιμήσω σε ("with honor I shall honor you").

**18** And-answered [va-ya'an] Balaam [Bil'am] and-said [va-yomer] unto [el] the-servants of [avdei] Balak [Balaq]: "If [im] Balak [Balaq] should-give [yitten] to-me [li] his-house [beito] full of [melo] silver [kesef] and-gold [ve-zahav], I-am-not-able [lo ukhal] to-transgress [la-avor] [et] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH my-God [Elohai], to-do [la-asot] a-small-thing [qetannah] or [o] a-great-thing [gedolah].

> **Key terms:** מְלֹא בֵיתוֹ כֶּסֶף וְזָהָב (melo veito kesef ve-zahav) = "the fullness of his house of silver and gold" — Balaam's declaration sounds noble but may be a negotiating stance (cf. the later narrative where YHWH's anger burns against him, v. 22). פִּי יְהוָה אֱלֹהָי (pi YHWH Elohai) = "the mouth of YHWH my God" — Balaam claims YHWH as "my God," a startling assertion from a Mesopotamian diviner. קְטַנָּה אוֹ גְדוֹלָה (qetannah o gedolah) = "a small thing or a great thing" — a merism expressing totality: nothing at all. **LXX:** ἐὰν δῷ μοι Βαλακ πλήρη τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ ἀργυρίου καὶ χρυσίου, οὐ δυνήσομαι παραβῆναι τὸ ῥῆμα κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ ("if Balak should give me his house full of silver and gold, I shall not be able to transgress the word of the Lord God").

**19** And-now [ve-attah], dwell [shevu] please [na] here [ba-zeh] also [gam] you [attem] the-night [ha-lailah], and-I-shall-know [ve-ed'ah] what [mah] YHWH shall-add [yosef] to-speak [dabber] with-me [immi]."

> **Key terms:** מַה־יֹּסֵף יְהוָה דַּבֵּר עִמִּי (mah-yosef YHWH dabber immi) = "what YHWH shall add to speak with me" — the verb יָסַף (yasaf, "to add/continue") suggests Balaam expects a further word beyond the initial prohibition. This second inquiry, after God has already spoken clearly, introduces the theological complexity of the passage — is Balaam genuinely seeking God's updated will, or is he hoping the answer will change? **LXX:** καὶ ὑπομείνατε αὐτοῦ καὶ ὑμεῖς τὴν νύκτα ταύτην, καὶ γνώσομαι τί προσθήσει κύριος λαλῆσαι πρός με ("and remain here, you also, this night, and I shall know what the Lord shall add to speak to me").

**20** And-came [va-yavo] God [Elohim] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am] at-night [lailah] and-said [va-yomer] to-him [lo]: "If [im] to-call [liqro] to-you [lekha] the-men [ha-anashim] have-come [ba'u], arise [qum], go [lekh] with-them [ittam]; and-only [ve-akh] [et] the-word [ha-davar] which [asher] I-shall-speak [adabber] unto-you [elekha], [et]-it [oto] you-shall-do [ta'aseh]."

> **Key terms:** God's second response conditionally permits the journey: אִם לִקְרֹא לְךָ בָּאוּ (im liqro lekha ba'u) = "if to call you the men have come" — the condition may imply that Balaam should go only if the summons is genuine and not merely for cursing. The restriction וְאַךְ (ve-akh, "and only/however") — the particle אַךְ always introduces a limitation — binds Balaam strictly to God's word. **LXX:** εἰ καλέσαι σε πάρεισιν οἱ ἄνθρωποι οὗτοι, ἀναστὰς ἀκολούθησον αὐτοῖς· ἀλλὰ τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐὰν λαλήσω πρὸς σέ, τοῦτο ποιήσεις ("if these men have come to call you, rise and follow them; but the word which I shall speak to you, this you shall do").

**21** And-rose [va-yaqom] Balaam [Bil'am] in-the-morning [ba-boqer] and-saddled [va-yakhavosh] [et] his-donkey [atono] and-went [va-yelekh] with [im] the-princes of [sarei] Moab [Mo'av].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ אֶת אֲתֹנוֹ (va-yakhavosh et atono) = "and he saddled his donkey" — the feminine noun אָתוֹן (aton, "she-donkey") is significant; this is the animal that will speak in vv. 28–30. The detail of Balaam personally saddling the donkey (rather than having a servant do it) recalls Abraham saddling his donkey before the binding of Isaac (Gen 22:3, same verb חבשׁ), a parallel the rabbis exploited (cf. Bereshit Rabbah 55:8). **LXX:** καὶ ἀναστὰς Βαλααμ τὸ πρωὶ ἐπέσαξεν τὴν ὄνον αὐτοῦ ("and Balaam having risen in the morning saddled his donkey").

---

## The Donkey and the Angel (vv. 22–35)

**22** And-burned [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] God [Elohim] because [ki] going [holekh] he [hu]; and-stationed-himself [va-yityatsev] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH in-the-road [ba-derekh] as-an-adversary [le-satan] to-him [lo]. And-he [ve-hu] [was] riding [rokhev] upon [al] his-donkey [atono], and-two [u-shnei] of-his-servants [ne'arav] [were] with-him [immo].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּחַר אַף אֱלֹהִים כִּי הוֹלֵךְ הוּא (va-yikhar af Elohim ki holekh hu) = "and the anger of God burned because going he [was]" — this is the crux of the narrative's theological tension: God has just given permission (v. 20), yet his anger burns at Balaam's going. The traditional resolution is that God permitted the journey but was angered by Balaam's eagerness or impure motive. לְשָׂטָן (le-satan) = "as an adversary/accuser" — the word שָׂטָן here is a common noun with the preposition ל, meaning "as an opponent," not a proper name. This is one of the earliest uses of the term in the Hebrew Bible. **LXX:** καὶ ὠργίσθη θυμῷ ὁ θεὸς ὅτι ἐπορεύθη αὐτός· καὶ ἀνέστη ὁ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐνδιαβάλλειν αὐτόν ("and God was angered with fury because he went; and the angel of God rose up to oppose him") — rendering לְשָׂטָן as ἐνδιαβάλλειν ("to accuse/oppose"), from which the word "devil" (διάβολος) derives.

**23** And-saw [va-tere] the-donkey [ha-aton] [et] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH standing [nitsav] in-the-road [ba-derekh] and-his-sword [ve-kharbo] drawn [shelufah] in-his-hand [be-yado], and-turned-aside [va-tet] the-donkey [ha-aton] from [min] the-road [ha-derekh] and-went [va-telekh] into-the-field [ba-sadeh]; and-struck [va-yakh] Balaam [Bil'am] [et] the-donkey [ha-aton] to-turn-her [le-hatotah] the-road [ha-derekh].

> **Key terms:** The verb וַתֵּרֶא (va-tere) = "and she saw" — the she-donkey perceives what the professional seer cannot. The irony is devastating: the animal whose name (אָתוֹן, aton) connotes lowliness sees the divine messenger, while the diviner (from the root "to see") is blind. חַרְבּוֹ שְׁלוּפָה (kharbo shelufah) = "his sword drawn" — the drawn sword indicates lethal intent (cf. Josh 5:13). **LXX:** καὶ ἰδοῦσα ἡ ὄνος τὸν ἄγγελον τοῦ θεοῦ ἀνθεστηκότα ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ καὶ τὴν ῥομφαίαν ἐσπασμένην ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ ("and the donkey having seen the angel of God standing in the road and his sword drawn in his hand").

**24** And-stood [va-ya'amod] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH in-a-path of [be-mish'ol] the-vineyards [ha-keramim], a-wall [gader] on-this-side [mi-zeh] and-a-wall [ve-gader] on-this-side [mi-zeh].

> **Key terms:** מִשְׁעוֹל הַכְּרָמִים (mish'ol ha-keramim) = "a path/lane of the vineyards" — a narrow way between vineyard walls. The setting progressively narrows: first an open road (v. 23), then a walled lane (v. 24), then a place with no room to turn (v. 26). The escalation mirrors the theological constriction closing around Balaam. גָּדֵר (gader) = "wall" — a low stone wall marking vineyard boundaries. **LXX:** καὶ ἔστη ὁ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν ταῖς αὔλαξιν τῶν ἀμπέλων, φραγμὸς ἐντεῦθεν καὶ φραγμὸς ἐντεῦθεν ("and the angel of God stood in the furrows of the vines, a fence on this side and a fence on that side").

**25** And-saw [va-tere] the-donkey [ha-aton] [et] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH, and-she-pressed [va-tillakhets] unto [el] the-wall [ha-qir] and-pressed [va-tilkhats] [et] the-foot of [regel] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] the-wall [ha-qir]; and-he-continued [va-yosef] to-strike-her [le-hakkotah].

> **Key terms:** וַתִּלָּחֵץ (va-tillakhets) = "and she pressed herself" — the niphal of לחץ (lakhats, "to press/squeeze"), describing the donkey squeezing against the wall in desperation to avoid the angel. רֶגֶל (regel) = "foot" — Balaam's foot is crushed against the wall, an increasingly painful physical consequence of his spiritual blindness. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδοῦσα ἡ ὄνος τὸν ἄγγελον τοῦ θεοῦ προσέθλιψεν ἑαυτὴν πρὸς τὸν τοῖχον ("and the donkey having seen the angel of God pressed herself against the wall").

**26** And-continued [va-yosef] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH to-pass-by [la-avor] and-stood [va-ya'amod] in-a-narrow [be-maqom tsar] place [maqom] where [asher] there-was-no [ein] road [derekh] to-turn [linot] right [yamin] or-left [u-smol].

> **Key terms:** מָקוֹם צָר (maqom tsar) = "a narrow place" — the word צָר (tsar, "narrow/distressing") echoes the root צרר used for "adversary" throughout Numbers. There is no escape: אֵין דֶּרֶךְ לִנְטוֹת יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול ("there was no road to turn right or left"). The triple narrowing — open road, walled lane, impassable constriction — is now complete. **LXX:** καὶ προσέθηκεν ὁ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ἀπελθὼν ὑπέστη ἐν τόπῳ στενῷ εἰς ὃν οὐκ ἦν ἐκκλῖναι δεξιὰν οὐδὲ ἀριστεράν ("and the angel of God went further and stood in a narrow place where there was no turning right or left").

**27** And-saw [va-tere] the-donkey [ha-aton] [et] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH, and-she-crouched [va-tirbats] beneath [takhat] Balaam [Bil'am]; and-burned [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-struck [va-yakh] [et] the-donkey [ha-aton] with-the-staff [ba-maqqel].

> **Key terms:** וַתִּרְבַּץ (va-tirbats) = "and she crouched/lay down" — from רבץ (ravats, "to lie down/crouch"), the posture of an animal refusing to move further. The donkey's progressive responses — turning aside (v. 23), pressing against the wall (v. 25), lying down (v. 27) — mirror the three-fold structure of the passage. The irony deepens: Balaam's anger (וַיִּחַר אַף) uses the same idiom as God's anger in v. 22. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδοῦσα ἡ ὄνος τὸν ἄγγελον τοῦ θεοῦ συνεκάθισεν ὑποκάτω Βαλααμ ("and the donkey having seen the angel of God sat down beneath Balaam").

**28** And-opened [va-yiftakh] YHWH [et] the-mouth of [pi] the-donkey [ha-aton], and-she-said [va-tomer] to-Balaam [le-Bil'am]: "What [meh] have-I-done [asiti] to-you [lakh] that [ki] you-have-struck-me [hikkitani] these [zeh] three [shalosh] times [regalim]?"

> **Key terms:** וַיִּפְתַּח יְהוָה אֶת פִּי הָאָתוֹן (va-yiftakh YHWH et pi ha-aton) = "and YHWH opened the mouth of the donkey" — the subject is YHWH (not Elohim), emphasizing that this is a direct miraculous act by Israel's covenant God. זֶה שָׁלֹשׁ רְגָלִים (zeh shalosh regalim) = "these three times" — the word רְגָלִים (regalim) can mean both "times/occurrences" and "feet" (as well as the technical term for the three pilgrimage festivals). The wordplay may be intentional: the donkey who saved Balaam with her feet (רֶגֶל, regel, v. 25) now complains about three "feet/times." **LXX:** καὶ ἤνοιξεν ὁ θεὸς τὸ στόμα τῆς ὄνου ("and God opened the mouth of the donkey") — Vaticanus reads ὁ θεός ("God") where the MT has YHWH.

**29** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] to-the-donkey [la-aton]: "Because [ki] you-have-dealt-wantonly [hit'allalt] against-me [bi]. Would-that [lu] there-were [yesh] a-sword [kherev] in-my-hand [be-yadi], for [ki] now [attah] I-would-have-killed-you [haragtikh]."

> **Key terms:** הִתְעַלַּלְתְּ (hit'allalt) = "you have dealt wantonly/made a fool of me" — from the hitpael of עלל (alal, "to abuse/deal capriciously"). The same root describes the plagues on Egypt (Exod 10:2). The supreme irony: Balaam wishes for a sword while an angel with a drawn sword stands before him unseen. לוּ יֶשׁ חֶרֶב בְּיָדִי (lu yesh kherev be-yadi) = "would that there were a sword in my hand" — a sword is exactly what the angel holds. **LXX:** ὅτι ἐμπέπαιχάς μοι· καὶ εἰ εἶχον μάχαιραν ἐν τῇ χειρί μου, ἤδη ἂν ἐξεκέντησά σε ("because you have mocked me; and if I had a sword in my hand, I would already have pierced you").

**30** And-said [va-tomer] the-donkey [ha-aton] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "Am-not [ha-lo] I [anokhi] your-donkey [atonkha] upon [asher] which [asher] you-have-ridden [rakhavta] upon-me [alai] from-ever-you-were [me-odkha] until [ad] this [ha-zeh] day [ha-yom]? Have-I-been-in-the-habit [ha-hasken hiskanti] of-doing [la-asot] thus [koh] to-you [lakh]?" And-he-said [va-yomer]: "No [lo]."

> **Key terms:** הַהַסְכֵּן הִסְכַּנְתִּי (ha-hasken hiskanti) = "have I been accustomed, have I been accustomed" — the infinitive absolute (הַסְכֵּן) with the finite verb (הִסְכַּנְתִּי) from סכן (sakhan, "to be accustomed/in the habit of"), forming the emphatic doubling construction. The donkey's argument is an appeal to established character: she has been Balaam's faithful mount his entire life. מֵעוֹדְךָ עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (me-odkha ad ha-yom ha-zeh) = "from ever-you-were until this day" — i.e., for the entire span of their relationship. Balaam's terse answer — לֹא (lo, "no") — is an admission that the donkey is behaving abnormally and that there must be a reason. **LXX:** ἡ ὄνος σου ἐφ᾿ ἧς ἐπέβαινες ἀπὸ νεότητός σου ἕως τῆς σήμερον ἡμέρας· μὴ ὑπεροψίᾳ ὑπερεῖδόν σε; ("your donkey upon which you have ridden from your youth until this day — have I ever disdainfully overlooked you?") — the LXX renders הִסְכַּנְתִּי with a different nuance, focusing on "disdain/contempt" (ὑπεροψία).

**31** And-uncovered [va-yegal] YHWH [et] the-eyes of [einei] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-saw [va-yar] [et] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH standing [nitsav] in-the-road [ba-derekh] and-his-sword [ve-kharbo] drawn [shelufah] in-his-hand [be-yado]; and-he-bowed [va-yiqqod] and-fell-on [va-yishtakhu] his-face [le-appav].

> **Key terms:** וַיְגַל יְהוָה אֶת עֵינֵי בִלְעָם (va-yegal YHWH et einei Bil'am) = "and YHWH uncovered the eyes of Balaam" — the piel of גלה (galah, "to uncover/reveal"). The seer's eyes must be opened by God before he can see what the donkey saw naturally. This is the theological center of the episode: true vision is a divine gift, not a professional skill. וַיִּקֹּד וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְאַפָּיו (va-yiqqod va-yishtakhu le-appav) = "and he bowed and fell on his face" — a double prostration indicating complete submission. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπεκάλυψεν ὁ θεὸς τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς Βαλααμ ("and God uncovered the eyes of Balaam").

**32** And-said [va-yomer] to-him [elav] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH: "Upon-what [al-mah] have-you-struck [hikkita] [et] your-donkey [atonkha] these [zeh] three [shalosh] times [regalim]? Behold [hinneh], I [anokhi] went-out [yatsati] as-an-adversary [le-satan], for [ki] the-road [ha-derekh] is-precipitous [yarat] before-me [le-negdi].

> **Key terms:** כִּי יָרַט הַדֶּרֶךְ לְנֶגְדִּי (ki yarat ha-derekh le-negdi) = "for the road is precipitous/reckless before me" — the rare verb יָרַט (yarat) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible (hapax legomenon). Its meaning is debated: "to be precipitous/reckless" (from ירט) or possibly "to rush headlong." The angel states that Balaam's path was recklessly contrary to the divine will. **LXX:** ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἐξῆλθον εἰς διαβολήν σου, ὅτι οὐκ ἀστεία ἡ ὁδός σου ἐναντίον μου ("behold, I went out to oppose you, for your road was not seemly/right before me").

**33** And-saw-me [va-tir'ani] the-donkey [ha-aton] and-turned-aside [va-tet] before-me [le-fanai] these [zeh] three [shalosh] times [regalim]. Perhaps [ulai] she-turned-aside [nathah] from-before-me [mippanai] — for [ki] now [attah] also [gam] you [otkha] I-would-have-killed [haragti], and-her [ve-otah] I-would-have-let-live [hekhe'eti]."

> **Key terms:** The angel's statement is shocking: had the donkey not turned aside, YHWH's angel would have killed Balaam and spared the donkey. The verb הֶחֱיֵיתִי (hekhe'eti) = "I would have let live" — the hiphil of חיה (khayah, "to live"), meaning "to preserve alive." The donkey saved Balaam's life three times, and he struck her three times — each blow was directed at his own savior. **LXX:** καὶ εἰ μὴ ἐξέκλινεν, νῦν οὖν σὲ μὲν ἀπέκτεινα, ἐκείνην δὲ περιεποιησάμην ("and if she had not turned aside, now I would have killed you and preserved her").

**34** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH: "I-have-sinned [khatati], for [ki] I-did-not [lo] know [yadati] that [ki] you [attah] [were] standing [nitsav] to-meet-me [liqrati] in-the-road [ba-derekh]. And-now [ve-attah], if [im] evil [ra] in-your-eyes [be-einekha], I-shall-return [ashuvah] to-me [li]."

> **Key terms:** חָטָאתִי (khatati) = "I have sinned" — Balaam's confession uses the standard verb for sin, חטא (khata', "to miss the mark/sin"). His plea אִם רַע בְּעֵינֶיךָ אָשׁוּבָה לִּי (im ra be-einekha ashuvah li) = "if evil in your eyes, I shall return for myself" — offers to abort the mission entirely. The irony of "in your eyes" (בְּעֵינֶיךָ) continues the seeing motif: Balaam whose eyes were closed now defers to the angel's sight. **LXX:** ἡμάρτηκα, οὐ γὰρ ἠπιστάμην ὅτι σύ μοι ἀνθέστηκας ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ ("I have sinned, for I did not know that you were opposing me in the road").

**35** And-said [va-yomer] the-angel of [mal'akh] YHWH unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "Go [lekh] with [im] the-men [ha-anashim]; and-only [ve-efes] the-word [ha-davar] which [asher] I-shall-speak [adabber] unto-you [elekha], [et]-it [oto] you-shall-speak [tedabber]." And-went [va-yelekh] Balaam [Bil'am] with [im] the-princes of [sarei] Balak [Balaq].

> **Key terms:** וְאֶפֶס (ve-efes) = "and only/but nothing except" — the particle אֶפֶס is stronger than אַךְ (akh) of v. 20, intensifying the restriction. God said אַךְ; the angel says אֶפֶס — the constraint tightens. Balaam may go, but he is now under even stricter divine control. The journey resumes, but on God's terms absolutely. **LXX:** πορεύου μετὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων· πλὴν τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐὰν εἴπω πρὸς σέ, τοῦτο φυλάξῃ λαλῆσαι ("go with the men; except the word which I shall speak to you, this you shall guard to speak").

---

## Balak Receives Balaam (vv. 36–41)

**36** And-heard [va-yishma] Balak [Balaq] that [ki] had-come [ba] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-went-out [va-yetse] to-meet-him [liqrato] unto [el] a-city of [ir] Moab [Mo'av] which [asher] [is] on [al] the-border of [gevul] the-Arnon [Arnon], which [asher] [is] on-the-end of [bi-qtseh] the-border [ha-gevul].

> **Key terms:** עִיר מוֹאָב (ir Mo'av) = "a city of Moab" — identified in context as a frontier city on the Arnon River (modern Wadi Mujib in Jordan), which formed the northern boundary of Moab. The double mention of גְּבוּל (gevul, "border/boundary") — "on the border of the Arnon, which is on the end of the border" — emphasizes the liminal character of the meeting place: Balak comes to the very edge of his territory to receive Balaam. **LXX:** καὶ ἀκούσας Βαλακ ὅτι ἥκει Βαλααμ, ἐξῆλθεν εἰς συνάντησιν αὐτῷ εἰς πόλιν Μωαβ, ἥ ἐστιν ἐπὶ τῶν ὁρίων Αρνων ("and Balak having heard that Balaam had come, went out to meet him at a city of Moab, which is on the borders of the Arnon").

**37** And-said [va-yomer] Balak [Balaq] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "Did-I-not [ha-lo] sending [shaloakh] I-sent [shalakhti] unto-you [elekha] to-call-you [liqro lakh]? Why [lammah] did-you-not [lo] come [halakhta] unto-me [elai]? Am-I-not-truly [ha-umnam lo] able [ukhal] to-honor-you [kabbedekha]?"

> **Key terms:** הֲלוֹא שָׁלֹחַ שָׁלַחְתִּי (ha-lo shaloakh shalakhti) = "did I not sending send" — the infinitive absolute (שָׁלֹחַ) with the finite verb (שָׁלַחְתִּי), expressing emphasis: "did I not surely send." Balak's tone is reproachful: he interprets Balaam's delayed arrival as an insult. הַאֻמְנָם לֹא אוּכַל כַּבְּדֶךָ (ha-umnam lo ukhal kabbedekha) = "am I truly not able to honor you?" — Balak assumes the issue is compensation. **LXX:** οὐχὶ ἀπέστειλα πρὸς σὲ καλέσαι σε; διὰ τί οὐκ ἤρχου πρός με; ὄντως οὐ δυνήσομαι τιμῆσαί σε; ("did I not send to call you? Why did you not come to me? Am I truly not able to honor you?").

**38** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Behold [hinneh], I-have-come [bati] unto-you [elekha]. Now [attah], being-able [yakol] am-I-able [ukhal] to-speak [dabber] anything [me'umah]? The-word [ha-davar] which [asher] God [Elohim] shall-put [yasim] in-my-mouth [be-fi], [et]-it [oto] I-shall-speak [adabber]."

> **Key terms:** הֲיָכוֹל אוּכַל דַּבֵּר מְאוּמָה (ha-yakhol ukhal dabber me'umah) = "being able, am I able to speak anything?" — another infinitive absolute construction (יָכוֹל + אוּכַל), this time forming a rhetorical question. Balaam disclaims any independent power of speech: he is a channel, not a source. הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר יָשִׂים אֱלֹהִים בְּפִי (ha-davar asher yasim Elohim be-fi) = "the word which God shall put in my mouth" — this becomes the controlling principle for all of Balaam's oracles in chapters 23–24. **LXX:** ἰδοὺ ἥκω πρὸς σέ· δυνατὸς ἔσομαι λαλῆσαί τι; τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐὰν βάλῃ ὁ θεὸς εἰς τὸ στόμα μου, τοῦτο λαλήσω ("behold, I have come to you; shall I be able to speak anything? The word which God shall put in my mouth, this I shall speak").

**39** And-went [va-yelekh] Balaam [Bil'am] with [im] Balak [Balaq], and-they-came [va-yavo'u] to-Kiriath-Huzoth [Qiryat Khutsot].

> **Key terms:** קִרְיַת חֻצוֹת (Qiryat Khutsot) = "City of Streets/Plazas" — the name suggests an urban center with open public spaces. Its exact location is unknown, but it was presumably in the interior of Moab, beyond the border town of v. 36. This is the staging ground for the sacrificial preparations. **LXX:** καὶ ἦλθεν εἰς πόλεις ἐπαύλεων ("and he came to cities of enclosures/courtyards") — reading חֻצוֹת as a common noun rather than a proper name.

**40** And-sacrificed [va-yizbakh] Balak [Balaq] cattle [baqar] and-flock [va-tson], and-he-sent [va-yeshallakh] to-Balaam [le-Bil'am] and-to-the-princes [ve-la-sarim] who [asher] [were] with-him [immo].

> **Key terms:** The sacrificial feast — בָּקָר וָצֹאן (baqar va-tson, "cattle and flock/sheep") — is a hospitality banquet and a cultic preparation. Balak sends portions (וַיְשַׁלַּח, va-yeshallakh, the piel of שׁלח, "to send") of the sacrificed animals to Balaam and the princes. This is standard ancient Near Eastern protocol: sharing sacrificial meat establishes the covenant relationship between host and guest before the divination ritual begins. **LXX:** καὶ ἔθυσεν Βαλακ πρόβατα καὶ μόσχους καὶ ἀπέστειλεν τῷ Βαλααμ καὶ τοῖς ἄρχουσι τοῖς μετ᾿ αὐτοῦ ("and Balak sacrificed sheep and calves and sent to Balaam and to the rulers who were with him").

**41** And-it-was [va-yehi] in-the-morning [ba-boqer], and-took [va-yiqqakh] Balak [Balaq] [et] Balaam [Bil'am] and-brought-him-up [va-ya'alehu] to-Bamoth-Baal [Bamot Ba'al]; and-he-saw [va-yar] from-there [mi-sham] the-end of [qetseh] the-people [ha-am].

> **Key terms:** בָּמוֹת בָּעַל (Bamot Ba'al) = "the High Places of Baal" — a Moabite cultic site dedicated to the storm deity Baal, strategically chosen because of its elevation for viewing Israel's encampment. קְצֵה הָעָם (qetseh ha-am) = "the end/edge of the people" — Balaam can see only a portion of the vast Israelite camp from this vantage point. This detail becomes significant in 23:13, where Balak will take Balaam to another location where he can see only "the end of them" — the partial view symbolizing Balak's hope that a limited perspective might enable a curse. The chapter ends on this precipice: Balaam looking down at Israel, poised between Balak's demand for a curse and YHWH's irrevocable blessing. **LXX:** καὶ ἐγενήθη πρωὶ καὶ παραλαβὼν Βαλακ τὸν Βαλααμ ἀνεβίβασεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὴν στήλην τοῦ Βααλ καὶ ἔδειξεν αὐτῷ ἐκεῖθεν μέρος τι τοῦ λαοῦ ("and it was in the morning, and Balak having taken Balaam brought him up to the pillar of Baal and showed him from there a part of the people") — rendering בָּמוֹת as στήλην ("pillar/stele") and interpreting קְצֵה as μέρος τι ("a certain part").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 174–176)

1. **Chapter location and parashah boundary:** Numbers 22 begins on page 174 of the Leningrad Codex (folio 48r, visible in the upper-left margin). The text spans three columns on this page. The parashah Balak (בָּלָק) — one of the traditional weekly Torah readings — begins at 22:2, marked by a parashah setumah (ס) following the itinerary notice of 22:1 (which belongs narratively to the preceding travel section, 21:10–20). The text continues through pages 175–176 (page 175 is the verso of folio 48, photographed inverted in the source image), concluding the chapter and extending into chapter 23.

2. **Scribal hand and correction marks:** The three pages exhibit the characteristic hand of Samuel ben Jacob, the scribe of the Leningrad Codex (completed ca. 1008–1009 CE in Cairo). The text is remarkably clean through this section, with no visible erasures, overwriting, or marginal corrections in the narrative portions. The Masoretic marginal notes (Masorah parva) are visible in the margins between the columns, providing word counts and cross-references.

3. **The ketiv/qere at v. 5:** The Leningrad Codex preserves the reading עַמּוֹ (ammo, "his people") in the body text (ketiv), though some manuscripts read עַמּוֹן (Ammon). The Masorah does not flag this as a ketiv/qere, supporting the reading "the land of the sons of his people" as the intended Masoretic text. The Samaritan Pentateuch also reads עַמּוֹ.

4. **Verse-division spacing:** Small spatial breaks within the column text mark the major narrative divisions: between v. 14 and v. 15 (the transition between the first and second embassy), and between v. 21 and v. 22 (the transition to the donkey episode). A further spatial break appears between v. 35 and v. 36 (the transition to the arrival in Moab).

5. **The divine names pattern:** The Leningrad Codex carefully preserves the alternation between YHWH and Elohim throughout the chapter. The narrator uses Elohim when God approaches Balaam (vv. 9, 10, 12, 20), YHWH when the angel acts (vv. 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 28, 31, 32, 34, 35), and YHWH in Balaam's own speech (vv. 8, 13, 18, 19). This pattern is faithfully maintained in the manuscript with no scribal confusion between the divine names.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pa_0129–0130)

6. **Chapter division and section markers:** The Vaticanus manuscript shows the chapter heading ΜΖ (= 47, reflecting the LXX's continuous chapter numbering from Leviticus into Numbers) visible at the left margin of pa_0129. The text runs in three columns of continuous scriptio continua (no word spacing), with occasional high stops and paragraph markers. The Balaam narrative begins partway through the first column.

7. **LXX rendering of the donkey's speech (v. 28):** The Vaticanus reads ὁ θεός ("God") where the MT has YHWH in the critical statement "And YHWH opened the mouth of the donkey." This substitution is typical of the LXX's tendency to use θεός and κύριος somewhat interchangeably, but here it softens the direct attribution to the covenant name.

8. **LXX at v. 20 — conditional permission:** The Vaticanus renders the Hebrew אִם לִקְרֹא לְךָ בָּאוּ ("if to call you they have come") as εἰ καλέσαι σε πάρεισιν ("if they are present to call you"), preserving the conditional force. This supports the reading that divine permission was conditional, not absolute — a point that helps resolve the apparent contradiction between v. 20 (permission) and v. 22 (divine anger).

9. **LXX "calf" for "ox" in v. 4:** The Vaticanus renders שׁוֹר (shor, "ox/bull") as μόσχος (moskhos, "calf/young bull") in Balak's simile about licking up the grass. This diminutive rendering may reflect the translator's theological tendency to reduce the power of the metaphor — a calf is less threatening than a full-grown ox — or may simply reflect Alexandrian Greek idiom for bovine imagery.

10. **LXX "pillar of Baal" for "High Places of Baal" in v. 41:** The Vaticanus renders בָּמוֹת בַּעַל (Bamot Ba'al, "High Places of Baal") as τὴν στήλην τοῦ Βααλ (tēn stēlēn tou Baal, "the pillar/stele of Baal"). This interpretive translation replaces the concept of elevated open-air worship sites (בָּמוֹת, bamot) with a specific cultic object (στήλη, stele) — perhaps reflecting the LXX translator's awareness of Moabite cultic pillars (such as the Mesha Stele tradition) or a desire to make the pagan worship more concrete for Greek readers.

### Theological and Structural Notes

11. **The three-fold pattern and the theology of sight:** The chapter is structured around repeated patterns of three: three divine encounters (vv. 9, 12, 20), three donkey episodes (vv. 23, 25, 27), three donkey-beatings, three speeches by the angel. The controlling theological theme is the reversal of sight: the professional seer (Balaam) is blind to the divine messenger, while the lowly animal sees clearly. YHWH must "uncover" (גלה) Balaam's eyes (v. 31), establishing that prophetic vision is not a human skill but a divine gift. This theme will carry through the oracles of chapters 23–24, where Balaam will declare himself "the man whose eye is opened" (24:3) — an ironic self-description given that his donkey's eyes were open before his.

12. **The satan-angel and the theology of divine opposition:** The angel of YHWH positions himself לְשָׂטָן (le-satan, "as an adversary") in v. 22 — using the word that will later develop into a title for the cosmic adversary (cf. Job 1–2; Zech 3:1–2; 1 Chr 21:1). Here, however, the satan-function is entirely positive: the angel opposes Balaam to save his life. The concept of divine opposition as salvific — God blocking a path that leads to destruction — is a significant theological motif. The narrow constriction of the road (open path > walled lane > impassable strait) mirrors the narrowing of Balaam's options until he has no choice but to submit to YHWH's will.

13. **Balaam's ambiguous character:** The chapter presents Balaam in deliberately complex terms. He invokes YHWH by name (v. 8), claims YHWH as "my God" (v. 18), and accurately reports YHWH's prohibitions (v. 13). Yet God's anger burns at his going (v. 22), and the angel nearly kills him (v. 33). The tension is never fully resolved in this chapter — it is the beginning of a characterization that develops across chapters 22–24 and reaches its devastating conclusion in 31:8, 16 where Balaam is killed and revealed as the author of the Baal-Peor apostasy. The rabbis crystallized this ambiguity in the phrase "Balaam the wicked" (בלעם הרשע), while also acknowledging that his oracles are among the most sublime prophecies in Torah.

14. **The hapax legomenon יָרַט (yarat) in v. 32:** The verb יָרַט occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its meaning must be inferred from context and cognate languages. Possible derivations include: (a) from an Aramaic root meaning "to rush/be precipitous"; (b) from Arabic waraTa, "to act rashly"; (c) from a hypothetical Hebrew root meaning "to be perverse/contrary." The ancient versions struggled with it: the LXX renders it as οὐκ ἀστεία ("not seemly/right"), the Vulgate as perversa ("perverse"), and the Targum Onqelos as סְטָת ("turned aside"). The Leningrad Codex vocalizes it as יָרַט with a patakh under the resh, supporting a qal perfect third-person form.
